We saw that a central task of sociology is to explore the interplay of society and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. full_notes:
Terms Concepts and Their Use in Sociology is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 24 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY CHAPTER 2 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY I INTRODUCTION The previous chapter introduced us to an idea both about society as well as sociology.

The text explains that we saw that a central task of sociology is to explore the interplay of society and the individual.

The text explains that we also saw that individuals do not float freely in society but are part of collective bodies like the family, tribe, caste, class, clan, nation.

The text explains that in this chapter, we move further to understand the kinds of groups individuals form, the kinds of unequal orders, stratification systems within which, individuals and groups are placed, the way social control operates, the roles that individuals have and play, and the status they occupy.

The text explains that in other words we start exploring how society itself functions.

The text explains that the question however remains as to why do we need specific terms and concepts to understand this.

The text explains that why does sociology need to have a special set of terms when we use terms like status and roles or social control anyway in our everyday life?

The text explains that for a discipline such as, say, nuclear physics that deals with matters unknown to most people and for which no word exists in common speech, it seems obvious that a discipline must develop a terminology.

The text explains that however, terminology is possibly even more important for sociology, just because its subject matter is familiar and just because words do exist to denote it.

The text explains that we are so well acquainted with the social institutions that surround us that we cannot see them clearly and precisely (Berger 1976:25).

The text explains that for example we may feel that since we live in families we know all about families.

The text explains that this would be conflating or equating sociological knowledge with common sense knowledge or naturalistic explanation, which we have discussed in Chapter 1.

The text explains that we also found in the previous chapter how sociology as a discipline

The text explains that 25 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY has a biography or history.

The text explains that we saw how certain material and intellectual developments shaped the sociological perspective as well as its concerns.

The text explains that likewise sociological concepts too have a story to tell.

The text explains that they therefore distinguished primary from secondary groups, community from society or association.

The text explains that other concepts like stratification reflect the concern that sociologists had in understanding the structured inequalities between groups in society.

The text explains that however just as there are different kinds of individuals and groups in society so there are different kinds of concepts and ideas.

The text explains that and sociology itself is marked by different ways of understanding society and looking at dramatic social changes that the modern period brought about.

The text explains that we have seen how even in the early stage of sociology’s emergence there were contrary and contesting understandings of society.

The text explains that if for Karl Marx class and conflict were key concepts to understand society, social solidarity and collective conscience were key terms for Emile Durkheim.

The text explains that in the Post-World War II period sociology was greatly influenced by the structural functionalists who found society essentially harmonious.

The text explains that they found it useful to compare society to an organism where different parts have a function to play for the maintenance of the whole.

The text explains that others, in particular the conflict theorists influenced by Marxism saw society as essentially conflict ridden.

The text explains that others began with macro structures such as class, caste, market, state or even community.

The text explains that they are keys to open locks to understand society.

The text explains that in sociology we both use and also constantly interrogate or question the concepts and categories.

The text explains that 26 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY II SOCIAL GROUPS AND SOCIETY Sociology is the study of human social life.

The text explains that a defining feature of human life is that humans interact, communicate and construct social collectivities.

The text explains that the first that in every society whether ancient or feudal or modern, Asian or European or African human groups and collectivities exist.

The text explains that what kind of groups are these? cannot but be otherwise for society itself is diverse.

The text explains that and how this very debate and discussion of differences helps us understand society.

The text explains that do they reflect different visions of what a good society ought to be like?

The text explains that 27 TERMS, CONCEPTS AND THEIR USE IN SOCIOLOGY A quasi group is an aggregate or combination, which lacks structure or organisation, and whose members may be unaware, or less aware, of the existence of groupings.

The text explains that a social group can be said to have at least the following characteristics : (i) persistent interaction to provide continuity; (ii) a stable pattern of these inter- actions; (iii) a sense of belonging to identify with other members, i.e. each individual is conscious of the group itself and its own set of rules, rituals and symbols; (iv) shared interest; (v) acceptance of common norms and values; and (vi) a definable structure.

The text explains that social structure here refers to patterns of regular and repetitive interaction between individuals or groups.

The text explains that a social group thus refers to a collection of continuously interacting persons who share common interest, culture, values and norms within a given society.

The text explains that schools, government offices, hospitals, students’ associations etc. are examples of secondary groups.

The text explains that community and Society or Association The idea of comparing and contrasting the old traditional and agrarian way of life with the new modern and urban one in terms of their different and contrasting social relationships and lifestyles, dates back to the writings of classical sociologists.

The text explains that the term ‘community’ refers to human relationships that are highly personal, intimate and enduring, those where a person’s involvement is considerable if not total, as in the family, with real friends or a close-knit group. ‘Society’ or ‘association’ refers to everything opposite of ‘community’, in particular the apparently impersonal, superficial and transitory relationships of modern urban life.

The text explains that but this process was gendered, i.e. it had different implications for men and women.

The text explains that peer pressure refers to the social pressure exerted by one’s peers on what one ought to do or not.

The text explains that why do we need to use special terms and concepts in sociology?

The text explains that as a member of society you must be interacting with and in different groups.

The text explains that how do you see these groups from a sociological perspective?

The text explains that what have you observed about the stratification system existing in your society?

The text explains that how are individual lives affected by stratification?

The text explains that do you think the modes of social control in different spheres of society are different?

The text explains that identify the different roles and status that you play and are located in.

The text explains that invitation to Sociology : A Humanistic Perspective.

The text explains that 2003. ‘Gender and the Khasi Family Structure : The Meghalaya Succession to Self-Acquired Property Act,1984’, in ed.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 2: Terms Concepts and Their Use in Sociology
